title: Module 4 Quiz
description: Test your understanding of flow, clutch, motivation, data, and mental fatigue.

blocks.content: Seven questions covering the core concepts from Module 4. This is retrieval practice — one of the most effective ways to lock knowledge into long-term memory.

blocks.question: 1. Match each state to its characteristic: Flow is ___. Clutch is ___.
blocks.explanation: Correct. Flow is effortless and automatic — you fall into it when conditions are right. Clutch is deliberate and intense — you choose it when the moment demands maximum effort.

options.text: Flow = effortless, automatic, 'letting it happen' / Clutch = deliberate, intense, 'making it happen'
Flow = deliberate, conscious, high effort / Clutch = effortless, unconscious, low effort
Flow = high pressure, time slows down / Clutch = low pressure, time speeds up
Flow = available on demand / Clutch = requires three specific triggers

blocks.question: 2. Select ALL three flow triggers:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Challenge-skills balance, clear goals, and unambiguous feedback. These are the three conditions you can engineer. The other six flow dimensions describe the experience, not the entry criteria.

blocks.question: 3. The burnout trajectory is:
blocks.explanation: Correct. The cascade is predictable: intrinsic motivation degrades into extrinsic when the sport becomes about proving rather than enjoying, then into amotivation (going through the motions), then burnout.

options.text: Intrinsic motivation → extrinsic motivation → amotivation → burnout
Obsessive passion → harmonious passion → amotivation → burnout
Ego orientation → task orientation → amotivation → burnout

blocks.question: 4. Interoceptive suppression occurs when:
blocks.explanation: Correct. When athletes habitually check external data, the neural pathways processing internal bodily signals get less practice and degrade. Use-it-or-lose-it. Analog Sessions are the rehabilitation.

options.text: Athletes suppress pain signals to continue racing
Coaches suppress athlete autonomy in training plans
Habitual data checking gradually atrophies the ability to assess effort internally

blocks.question: 5. Off-Day Protocol Step 1: Name it — 'This is ___, not laziness.'
blocks.explanation: Correct. 'This is mental fatigue, not laziness.' The reframe matters — the character judgment adds guilt, which adds cognitive load, which makes the adenosine problem worse. Naming it accurately breaks the spiral.

blocks.question: 6. Match each SDT need to its sport example: An athlete who modifies their own workout plan is exercising ___. An athlete who hits benchmarks they couldn't hit 6 months ago is satisfying ___. An athlete who trains with a partner instead of solo is meeting ___.
blocks.explanation: Correct. Modifying your own plan = autonomy (choice and control). Hitting new benchmarks = competence (getting better). Training with a partner = relatedness (connection to others).

blocks.question: 7. Scenario: You're in a flow state during a long ride. You glance at your power meter and see you're 15W below target. The flow breaks. What's the best response?
blocks.explanation: Correct. Data should confirm what your body already told you, not override it. Mid-flow data checks are one of the 16 technostress factors Bischof identified. Trust the internal signal, use data at natural breakpoints, and consider Analog Sessions to rebuild interoception.

options.text: Hammer to hit the target number — the data knows better than feel
Stop training with power meters entirely — data always hurts performance
Note the discrepancy, trust the feel-based signal, and consider checking data only at natural breaks to protect flow states
Recalibrate your power meter immediately — the device must be wrong
